completeness.rationale: Six of seven expected extracts are present: task count (~5,000), title-fast/description-slow distinction, v2.2-to-v2.3 regression, ~2 weeks ago onset, 10-15 second timing, and long descriptions. CPU spiking to 100% during searches was never asked about and is absent from the summary.

accuracy.rationale: The triage correctly identifies both acceptable diagnostic paths simultaneously: the v2.3 upgrade as the trigger and the title-vs-description performance gap pointing to an indexing issue. The root cause hypothesis explicitly names 'a change from indexed full-text search to unindexed LIKE/regex scanning' — which is precisely the actual cause. No inaccuracies.

thoroughness.rationale: The agent covered the key diagnostic dimensions: search type, scale, version correlation, query variation, consistency, and description length. CPU behavior during search — a natural follow-up for a performance regression — was skipped. OS and database backend were noted as gaps but not asked. These omissions are acknowledged in information_gaps, so the agent was self-aware, but the CPU question in particular was an obvious missed probe.

economy.rationale: Three turns, all productive. The workaround question in turn 3 added mild value to the report but wasn't strictly diagnostic. Everything else was well-targeted. No redundancy or repeated asks.

question_quality.rationale: Turn 2 was the standout: asking whether 'certain kinds' of searches are slow — specifically single-word vs. complex — prompted the reporter to notice the title/description distinction entirely on their own. This is genuine diagnostic reasoning that surfaced the key insight without leading the witness. Turn 3 then correctly probed whether description length was a compounding factor.

actionability.rationale: The summary gives a developer an immediate starting point: diff search implementation between v2.2 and v2.3, run EXPLAIN/ANALYZE on description-matching queries, check for a missing FTS index. Reproduction steps are specific and realistic. A proposed regression test case is included. A developer could open the codebase and begin investigating right now.

notable_strengths: Turn 2 question about 'certain kinds' of searches was genuinely insightful — it prompted the reporter to independently discover the title-vs-description distinction, which is the crux of the bug
Root cause hypothesis names the exact mechanism (FTS index replaced by LIKE/regex scanning) without the agent having direct access to the code
The triage summary is exceptionally well-structured: problem, hypothesis, reproduction steps, fix guidance, proposed test, and acknowledged gaps all in one artifact
notable_weaknesses: CPU behavior during search was never asked about — a natural probe for any performance regression that would have confirmed a computational bottleneck
OS and database backend were recognized as gaps but not pursued during questioning; asking early could have sharpened the hypothesis
most_insightful_question: Does it happen with every search you run, or only certain kinds? For example, is a single-word search like 'meeting' just as slow as a longer or more complex query?
missed_opportunities: Ask whether CPU or memory usage spikes visibly during slow searches (would confirm computational bottleneck vs. network/IO)
Ask what OS and TaskFlow client (desktop app, browser, mobile) they are using — noted as a gap but never asked
Ask whether they had tried any cache clearing or reindexing since the upgrade — the ground truth confirms they hadn't, which is relevant for a workaround
